name: Haptics & Micro-interactions
overview: Haptics and micro-interactions are the small, single-purpose moments where the system acknowledges a user action through tactile (Taptic Engine), visual (icon bounce, color shift), and audio feedback. The canonical framework comes from Dan Saffer's Microinteractions (2013) - trigger / rules / feedback / loops & modes - and the canonical functional test from Stripe's Michael Villar (2014): 'If you disable animations, the flow should feel broken; if it is not, your animations are superfluous.' Modern Apple platforms collapse this to one-line implementations (.sensoryFeedback(.success, trigger:) and .symbolEffect(.bounce)) and HIG explicitly warns against overuse, late-night nudges, and dopamine-hijacking patterns. The pattern is the lowest-cost-per-delight primitive available to Apple-native apps and the easiest place to ruin a brand's perceived calm if abused.
originator_or_company: Theory: Dan Saffer (Designing for Interactions, Microinteractions: Designing with Details, O'Reilly 2013, with foreword by Don Norman). Practice canon: Michael Villar (ex-Stripe Checkout designer, now independent, michaelvillar.com) for the 'turn it off and see if the flow breaks' principle. Animation-as-meaning canon: Val Head (Adobe Principal Designer, Designing Interface Animation, Rosenfeld 2016). Modern interaction-design voice: Rauno Freiberg (Staff Design Engineer, Vercel; previously The Browser Company / Arc; runs Devouring Details course). Platform owner: Apple (Human Interface Guidelines: Playing haptics, Motion; Core Haptics framework 2019; SwiftUI .sensoryFeedback iOS 17 2023; SF Symbols 5 .symbolEffect iOS 17 2023; Audio Haptics in iOS 18 2024).
key_resources: [Improve the payment experience with animations - Michael Villar / Stripe (2014, source of the 'turn off animations' test)](https://medium.com/bridge-collection/improve-the-payment-experience-with-animations-3d1b0a9b810e)
[Microinteractions: Designing with Details - Dan Saffer, O'Reilly 2013](https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/microinteractions/9781449342760/)
[Animate symbols in your app - WWDC23 Session 10258](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2023/10258/)
[Devouring Details - Rauno Freiberg's interaction design course](https://devouringdetails.com/)
[Sensory feedback in SwiftUI - Swift with Majid (2023)](https://swiftwithmajid.com/2023/10/10/sensory-feedback-in-swiftui/)
[How to animate SF Symbols - Hacking with Swift](https://www.hackingwithswift.com/quick-start/swiftui/how-to-animate-sf-symbols)
[Understanding Success Criterion 2.3.3: Animation from Interactions - W3C WCAG 2.2](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/animation-from-interactions.html)
[Haptic Rewards: How Mobile Vibrations Shape Reward Response - Journal of Consumer Research (2024)](https://academic.oup.com/jcr/advance-article/doi/10.1093/jcr/ucaf025/8120234)
core_mechanics: (1) SAFFER'S 4-PART STRUCTURE - every micro-interaction has: (a) Trigger (manual: tap/swipe; system: state change), (b) Rules (what's allowed mid-interaction), (c) Feedback (visual + haptic + audio confirming the rule fired), (d) Loops & Modes (how it repeats / changes over time). (2) APPLE'S 3 HAPTIC FAMILIES (UIFeedbackGenerator since iOS 10, .sensoryFeedback since iOS 17): NOTIFICATION (.success, .warning, .error - ~0.4s patterns reserved for transactional outcomes), IMPACT (.light/.medium/.heavy/.soft/.rigid - sub-100ms collisions, e.g. button taps, card snaps), SELECTION (single tick on value change, e.g. picker, slider). (3) ONE-LINE IMPLEMENTATION - SwiftUI: .sensoryFeedback(.success, trigger: isCorrect) attaches to any view and fires when the trigger Equatable changes; this replaces 5-10 lines of UIImpactFeedbackGenerator boilerplate. SF Symbols 5: .symbolEffect(.bounce, value: counter) for one-shot bounce, .symbolEffect(.pulse) for indefinite, .symbolEffect(.variableColor.iterative.reversing) for layered fades, .contentTransition(.symbolEffect(.replace)) for smooth icon swaps. (4) STRIPE / VILLAR FUNCTIONAL TEST - the canonical disambiguation between functional and decorative motion: turn the animation off; if the flow still works perfectly, the animation was decoration; if the flow breaks, the animation was carrying meaning (e.g. spatial origin of a sheet, parent-child relationship in a transition). (5) TIMING BUDGETS - tap acknowledgment <100ms (perceived as instant), micro-interaction completion 200-400ms, transitions 300-500ms; anything >800ms feels sluggish per Apple HIG / Material Design / Nielsen Norman heuristics. (6) HAPTIC-VISUAL-AUDIO COUPLING - the strongest perceived 'quality' comes from synchronized visual+haptic+optional sound (Apple's switch toggle, watchOS ring closure). Mismatch (haptic without visual, or visual without haptic where one is expected) reads as broken. (7) RAUNO FREIBERG'S CRAFT NOTES - apply Disney's 12 principles, esp. follow-through and overlapping action, to UI; build interactions in code (not Figma) because timing/easing must be felt; robustness > novelty (a 'works 80% of the time' interaction destroys quality perception). (8) REDUCED MOTION - prefers-reduced-motion / UIAccessibility.isReduceMotionEnabled must replace any spring/parallax with a fade or instant change; iOS exposes this automatically to SwiftUI animations marked .accessibilityReduceMotion-aware. (9) HAPTIC SUPPRESSION CONDITIONS (iOS) - Low Power Mode, camera active, dictation active, system Settings haptics off, ringer switch off (some patterns); apps must degrade gracefully and never rely on haptic alone to convey information. (10) HAPTICS BUDGET - Apple HIG: 'Don't overuse haptics'; one rule of thumb (BiTE Interactive, Apple Developer forum lore) is < ~10 haptic events per minute of active use, with the strongest patterns (.heavy, .success/.error) reserved for outcome-bearing moments only. (11) AUDIO HAPTICS (iOS 18, 2024) - new ability to sync haptics to audio waveforms (CHHapticEngine + AHAP files), used for music apps and game beats; for productivity apps this is mostly out of scope.
designer_or_researcher_voice: Michael Villar (Stripe Checkout, 2014, Medium): 'If you disable animations, the flow should feel broken; if it is not, this might mean your animations are superfluous.'

Michael Villar (Stripe Checkout, 2014, Medium): 'The goal of animations should not solely be entertaining the user, but rather, they should be helping the user better understand what's going on and how to use your product most effectively.'

Dan Saffer (Microinteractions, O'Reilly 2013): 'Microinteractions can change a product from one that's tolerated into one that's treasured.' Saffer's central thesis: every micro-interaction is built from Trigger -> Rules -> Feedback -> Loops & Modes, and most product 'feel' lives in details no roadmap line item names.

Val Head (Designing Interface Animation, 2016): 'The very first rule of interface animation is that all interface animations must have a purpose for being there. The only purpose that isn't allowed is delight all on its own - delight isn't something that can be created by animation alone, and an animation added solely to increase delight usually does exactly the opposite from the user's perspective.'

Rauno Freiberg (Vercel Staff Design Engineer, ui.land interview): 'If your UI only works 80% of the time, the perception of quality breaks. Core interactions like scrolling, text input, and navigation must always work perfectly.' Rauno also names Disney's 'Follow-Through and Overlapping Action' as his single favorite UI principle, and refuses to design interactions in Figma because timing must be felt in code.

Apple HIG (Playing haptics): 'Use haptics consistently. It's important to build a clear, causal relationship between each haptic and its trigger so people learn to associate certain haptic patterns with certain experiences. Don't overuse haptics. Sparing use of haptics can make a meaningful impact when it occurs - too-frequent haptics can dilute meaning and annoy.'

Apple HIG (Motion): 'If motion itself conveys meaning - such as a status change or a hierarchical context transition - don't remove the animation entirely [under Reduce Motion]. Substitute a more subtle effect like a dissolve, highlight fade, or color shift.'
metrics_or_evidence: Journal of Consumer Research (Oxford, 2024, 'Haptic Rewards: How Mobile Vibrations Shape Reward Response and Consumer Choice'): mobile vibrations function as a secondary classical-conditioning reward, reinforcing the action-vibration association and raising re-engagement likelihood; effect attenuates with overuse (haptic fatigue documented). MoldStud / industry benchmark cited in Apple HIG-derived implementation studies: properly tuned haptic confirmation paired with visual feedback within 100ms improves task-completion accuracy ~18% in controlled lab settings (note: secondary citation, not a published RCT - flag as observational). Duolingo design team blog: streak-extension haptic + Duo phoenix animation are explicitly engineered as 'haptic rewards to keep users coming back', credited internally with double-digit DAU lifts during streak campaigns (company case study, no public n). Apple Heart & Movement Study + watchOS ring-close haptic: ring-close chime + Taptic notification associated with strong user-reported satisfaction, though no isolated RCT separates haptic from visual contribution. Reduce Motion adoption (Apple developer outreach): single-digit % of iOS users have Reduce Motion enabled but ratio skews higher in 30+ female demographic and vestibular-sensitive populations; not designing for it is a documented App Store accessibility-evaluation failure mode.
criticisms: (1) HAPTIC FATIGUE - the most cited failure mode (HackerNoon, BiTE Interactive, multiple Reddit/r/iOSProgramming threads): apps that buzz on every tap desensitize users so the haptic stops conveying meaning; many users globally turn off System Haptics rather than per-app, harming the entire ecosystem. (2) DOPAMINE LOOP RISK - JCR 2024 frames mobile vibration as classical-conditioning reward; weaponized in slot-machine UIs, infinite-scroll feeds, and gambling apps. The same mechanism that makes a correct-answer haptic satisfying can engineer compulsion if attached to variable rewards or streak-loss aversion. Center for Humane Technology flags this as a stack with notification scarcity + variable rewards. (3) DELIGHT-ONLY ANIMATION TRAP - Val Head's most-quoted warning: animation added 'just for delight' usually subtracts from delight; users perceive it as slow, gratuitous, or 'show-offy'. (4) NOVELTY EFFECT - Rauno Freiberg's robustness critique: a clever animation that fails under real-world conditions (slow device, low memory, mid-scroll interruption) destroys trust faster than no animation would have. (5) ACCESSIBILITY GAPS - failing to honor prefers-reduced-motion / UIAccessibility.isReduceMotionEnabled triggers vestibular symptoms (dizziness, nausea, headache) per W3C WCAG 2.2 SC 2.3.3 AAA; Apple's App Store Reduced Motion evaluation criteria explicitly check this. (6) HAPTIC-AS-INFORMATION FAILURE - relying on haptic alone to convey state (e.g. 'you got it wrong, only haptic, no visual') excludes deaf-blind users, users in Low Power Mode, and users on devices without Taptic Engine; HIG mandates haptic only as REINFORCEMENT, never as sole channel. (7) THEORETICAL EVIDENCE BASE - Saffer's 4-part framework is taxonomy, not RCT-validated; Villar's principle is a heuristic, not a measured law; the strongest evidence is observational/anecdotal across product case studies.
failure_cases: (1) MS WINDOWS XP / VISTA EXCESSIVE ANIMATION - menu fades, shadow draws, zoom-in maximizes; widely disabled by users, became cultural shorthand for 'animation as obstruction', informed Apple's later restraint. (2) FACEBOOK / META OVER-HAPTIC NEWSFEED PERIOD (mid-2010s) - frequent vibration on every reaction button drove users to disable haptics globally; Meta later dialed back. (3) IOS 8 STAGE PARALLAX (2014) - 'Reduce Motion' setting was added precisely because launch-pad parallax and zoom-on-app-open caused vestibular complaints; the very existence of Reduce Motion is a failure-case patch. (4) DUOLINGO LEAGUES / STREAK FREEZE INTERSECTION - reported user testimonies (dyniol.com, Duolingo subreddit) of users 'haptically conditioned' to open the app at 11:55pm to preserve streak, classic Means-Ends Reversal. Duolingo has tuned mascot animation toward 'frustration after wrong answers' - some users report this reads as guilt-tripping rather than encouraging. (5) APPLE WATCH PRE-WATCHOS-11 - constant Stand and Move haptic nudges late in the evening were widely turned off; Apple shipped Pause Rings + per-day-of-week goals only in 2024 after years of complaints. (6) ANY APP THAT SHIPS HAPTICS WITHOUT A TOGGLE - HIG-compliance failure; some App Review rejections cite this for accessibility. (7) STRIPE'S OWN PRE-2014 CHECKOUT - Villar's article frames the redesign as a response to a previous version where animations were inconsistent and users didn't follow the spatial logic of the form; the article documents the pivot to functional motion.
antipatterns: (1) DON'T attach .success haptic to a streak-extension event paired with loss-aversion ('don't break your streak!') - that combination is the Center for Humane Technology compulsion stack and trades long-term trust for short-term DAU. (2) DON'T fire haptics on routine taps (every list-row press, every text-field focus) - haptic fatigue, users disable system-wide. (3) DON'T use .heavy or .error haptics for non-error events - dilutes the semantic vocabulary; users no longer trust .error means error. (4) DON'T animate without honoring UIAccessibility.isReduceMotionEnabled / @Environment(\.accessibilityReduceMotion) - WCAG 2.3.3 violation, Apple App Store accessibility-evaluation flag. (5) DON'T rely on haptic alone (no visual + no audio + no text) to convey information - excludes Low Power Mode users, vibration-disabled users, deaf-blind users. (6) DON'T tie a haptic to monetization or paywall ('feel that buzz, that's your trial expiring') - reads as predatory. (7) DON'T mix custom Core Haptics waveforms that fight system patterns - users have learned the system .success / .error vocabulary; a 'creative' custom buzz for success is a UX regression. (8) DON'T animate to >800ms for a routine transition - feels sluggish even if pretty. (9) DON'T use Stripe-Villar logic backwards - 'animations are essential to flow' is NOT permission to add motion everywhere; it's a NECESSITY test for motion already proposed. (10) DON'T use SF Symbols .pulse indefinite effect on persistent UI - exhausting attention budget, eventually ignored.
applicability_to_shikaq: VERY HIGH fit, S-to-M implementation cost, biggest leverage-per-effort in Phase 1. shikaq's Apple-HIG-all-in design philosophy makes haptics + .symbolEffect the literal Apple-native way to express 'small wins' that the 20s-female / iPad-cafe persona expects from a polished iOS app. Translation playbook: (a) HAPTIC SEMANTIC MAP for shikaq - .selection on choice tap (sub-100ms tick); .impact(weight: .medium) on submit-answer; .success on correct + ring fill; .warning (NOT .error) on wrong (Duolingo's psychological-safety lesson - never red-screen a learner); .success on session complete + ring close. (b) SYMBOL-EFFECT SEMANTIC MAP - checkmark.circle.fill .symbolEffect(.bounce.up) on correct; xmark.circle (Material Symbols equivalent) gentle .pulse fade on wrong; flame.fill .symbolEffect(.variableColor.iterative.reversing) on streak rendering only at session end (never in passive Sidebar - exhausts attention); ring-close uses spring expand (already in components/ActivityRings.tsx). (c) VILLAR TEST APPLIED to shikaq - turn off all animation: does the answer-submit -> result -> next-question flow still work? Yes mostly, but the spatial origin of 'next question slides in from right' carries learning-relevant meaning (forward progress); keep that, drop pure decoration. (d) REACT NATIVE PARITY via expo-haptics on native + WebHaptics fallback (Vibration API) on web; abstract to a single hook useHaptic() so call sites read like SwiftUI's one-line modifier. (e) REDUCE-MOTION PATH - read AccessibilityInfo.isReduceMotionEnabled (RN) / prefers-reduced-motion (web), substitute spring with linear opacity fade, KEEP haptic (haptic is not motion-disabled by Reduce Motion - it's a separate System Haptics toggle). (f) NEVER late-night ('still time to close your ring') haptic nudge; morning 5問 + evening voice quiz pattern from CLAUDE.md handles cadence. (g) NEVER a custom branded haptic waveform - lean entirely on system .success / .selection / .impact vocabulary, that's the HIG-canon move. HEXAD coverage: Achiever (sub-100ms confirmation reinforces small wins), Free Spirit (haptic toggle in settings), Player (subtle game-feel without manipulation), Philanthropist N/A, Disruptor N/A (and good - we don't want to optimize for them).
phase_assignment: Phase 1 (3 weeks). Single highest leverage-per-effort primitive available. Ship: useHaptic() hook with .selection / .impact / .success / .warning, attach to PracticeScreen submit button + correct/wrong reveal + session-complete; SymbolEffect-equivalent (Reanimated spring on Material Symbols + opacity pulse) for checkmark / xmark / flame on session-end only; system-Reduce-Motion respect; system-Haptics-toggle respect; in-app per-user 'Haptics: System / Reduced / Off' setting in profile. Phase 2 reserves any audio-haptic synchronization, AHAP custom patterns, and onboarding micro-interaction polish. Phase 3 is for any non-essential 'delight' moments (year-end recap animation, etc.).
integration_points: Existing components: components/ActivityRings.tsx and components/GoalRing.tsx already render Reanimated springs - add haptic on ring-close; components/MaterialIcon.tsx is the chokepoint to add a transient .pulse / .bounce equivalent (Reanimated withSpring scale 1 -> 1.15 -> 1, 280ms total, damping 16, stiffness 400) controlled by an `animateOnMount` or `pulseKey` prop; components/screens/Practice.tsx (assumed) is the primary call site - on choice tap = .selection, on submit = .impact medium, on result reveal = .success / .warning, on session complete = .success + ring-close animation; components/TodayContent.tsx adds streak/ring-close haptic chain on first daily ring closure. New hook: lib/useHaptic.ts wrapping expo-haptics with web Vibration API fallback and reading user preference. New module: lib/motion.ts exporting reusable spring presets that respect AccessibilityInfo.isReduceMotionEnabled. DB: profiles add `haptics_preference: 'system' | 'reduced' | 'off'` column. Routes: settings -> Sounds & Haptics screen (Apple Settings parallel). Tailwind/NativeWind: no changes - motion is animated values, not classes. Sentry: instrument haptic-trigger errors (rare but Low Power Mode edge cases) at debug level, not error.
risks: (1) HAPTIC FATIGUE if call sites multiply unchecked - mitigation: code-review rule 'one .success per session ceiling, one .selection per tap', enforce by lint or PR template. (2) PERSONA MISMATCH for users with vibration-sensitivity or anxiety conditions - mitigation: ship in-app 'Haptics off' toggle from v1, default to 'System' (lets system-level setting win). (3) WEB FALLBACK GAP - Vibration API has weak/absent support on iOS Safari (PWA target) and inconsistent on Android Chrome; native iOS PWA support effectively zero; mitigation: web build downgrades haptic to no-op silently. (4) SCOPE CREEP into custom Core Haptics AHAP patterns - tempting once team learns the API, but maintenance + accessibility surface explodes; mitigation: charter rule - shikaq uses ONLY system .success / .selection / .impact / .warning. (5) MEANS-ENDS REVERSAL - if engagement metrics start optimizing for 'haptic-triggered sessions per day', the team unconsciously inflates haptic frequency; mitigation: do not put haptic-firing rate in any KPI dashboard. (6) ANDROID PARITY - expo-haptics on Android is just Vibrator with crude long/short, not Taptic-quality; risk of degraded Android UX feeling 'cheap'; mitigation: accept and document, given iPad-first persona. (7) iOS 17 BASELINE - .sensoryFeedback and .symbolEffect require iOS 17+; if shikaq supports iOS 16 (RN side), need UIFeedbackGenerator fallback - already what expo-haptics does internally, low risk.
apple_hig_compatibility: compatible. Haptics + micro-interactions are explicit, first-class HIG topics with their own pages (Playing haptics, Motion); .sensoryFeedback (iOS 17) and .symbolEffect (iOS 17, SF Symbols 5) are Apple's literally-one-line APIs precisely so developers stop building custom UIFeedbackGenerator boilerplate. The ONLY adaptation needed for shikaq is React Native parity via expo-haptics + Reanimated, which is well-trodden. shikaq design rules (Liquid Glass deference, SF Pro hierarchy, reserved Apple semantics) all align with HIG haptic restraint. Zero conflicts with Material Symbols choice - Material Symbols icons can still receive Reanimated spring transforms equivalent to .symbolEffect. Caveat: HIG forbids relying on haptic alone to convey state, so every haptic in shikaq must be paired with visual feedback (which we already plan).
ethical_score: neutral, leaning positive WITH guardrails, leaning negative without. Positive levers: a calm sub-100ms haptic + checkmark bounce on a correct answer is a textbook Self-Determination-Theory competence affordance, no scarcity, no loss-aversion, no late-night manipulation. Negative levers (must NOT ship): pairing haptic with streak-loss aversion ('feel the buzz of your streak ending'), variable-reward random haptic patterns, late-night haptic nudges, haptic on monetization / paywall events. Center-for-Humane-Technology score is clearly positive when shikaq adopts: in-app haptics toggle from v1, no late-night nudges, haptic vocabulary limited to system .success / .selection / .impact / .warning, never .error on wrong-answer (psychological safety), no haptic-driven streak shaming.
evidence_quality: mixed. Theoretical (Saffer's 4-part taxonomy, Val Head's purposefulness rule, Villar's heuristic) is well-cited but not RCT-validated. Observational_study: JCR 2024 'Haptic Rewards' on classical-conditioning effect of mobile vibrations is peer-reviewed; Apple's own internal usability data referenced in WWDC sessions (18, 19, 23) is company-reported. Company_case_study: Stripe Checkout (Villar 2014), Duolingo streak animation blog, Apple Watch ring closure are all well-documented but not independent. Anecdote: a large body of practitioner blogs (Hacking with Swift, Sarunw, Use Your Loaf, AppCoda, Devouring Details) on implementation. RCT-grade evidence isolating haptic-from-visual contribution is essentially absent in published literature.
localization_fit_japan: high. Japanese 20s-female users overwhelmingly own iPhones (>70% iOS share JP, female 20s skews higher) and have already internalized iOS system haptic vocabulary - .selection on Picker, .success on Apple Pay, .impact on swipe-to-delete are baseline expectations. Apple's quietness aesthetic ('refined restraint', 上品 / 控えめ) maps directly onto haptic restraint - Japanese users particularly punish overuse (cf. Japanese App Store reviews of noisy/buzzy apps). The 'morning 5問 / evening voice quiz' shikaq cadence already aligns with the 続ける文化 / 丁寧 register; haptics added at session-bookend moments rather than every tap reinforces the quiet competence vibe. Risk: zero - Japan is the strongest market for understated haptic micro-interactions globally, and the iPad-cafe persona's primary benchmark apps (Apple Notes, Apple Books, Notion JP, スタディサプリ when restrained) all use this exact pattern.
